introSection: Greezed Lightnin' was a steel launched shuttle roller coaster located at Six Flags AstroWorld in Houston, Texas, USA.

sections.title: History
sections.content:
The roller coaster first opened in 1978 as part of the Western Junction area. The following year the ride's queue received air operated gates.

In 1983, Greezed Lightnin' received re-profiled track and a modified drive panel.

In 1997, Greezed Lightnin' was given a new computer system.

In 2000, the ride received a good refurbishment, with the track and trains being repainted. A new flywheel was also installed, along with a "stop-light" launch system and a Allen-Bradly automation system. The theming was also renovated.

In 2002, Greezed Lightnin' received an ACE plaque honoring the late Anton Schwarzkopf.

In 2003, Greezed Lightnin' celebrated its 25th anniversary.

In 2005, Greezed Lightnin', along with Viper, received seat dividers.

On October 15, 2005, Greezed Lightnin' celebrated its one millionth launch.

It would continue to operate until the permanent closure of Six Flags AstroWorld on October 30, 2005. It was then dismantled.

On January 5, 2006, the ride's control room caught on fire during the ride's disassembly. A section of the queue and station building were destroyed. Extremely hot equipment used for the ride's disassembly was blamed for the fire. The ride suffered no damage.

In January 2006, the ride was sold at auction for $100,000 to David Dean, the co-owner of Joyland Amusement Park. Its mechanical parts and train were planned to be refurbished by Larson International. It was placed in storage about a mile north of Joyland Amusement Park in Lubbock, Texas, USA.

The park originally hoped to reopen the coaster in 2007, but due to a construction delay revealing that the area chosen for installation had wet soil and water four feet under the ground which led to increased cost, the ride was delayed for a 2008 reopening. This would later be pushed back to 2009 following further problems.

The park found it difficult to rebuild the coaster at the park due to the water table the park was located over.

In November 2009, the park abandoned plans to install the ride and listed it for sale for $399,000 on a used ride site. This was later reduced to $299,000 by January 2011.

In 2012, it was announced that Cliff's Amusement Park in Albuquerque, New Mexico, USA would be receiving the roller coaster in exchange for a used SCS waterplay unit going to Joyland, however, the sale was believed to have never took place.

As part of the proposed Grand Texas theme park, Greezed Lightnin' was also to be refurbished and reopened. This also fell through.

In November 2017, the train from Greezed Lightnin' was sold to Frontier City for use on Silver Bullet. The rest of the ride has since been scrapped, with the majority of it being hauled away by late 2018. Only a few remaining parts remain today.

sections.title: Design
sections.content: The ride had a single vertical loop which the train passes through forwards and backwards.

sections.title: External links
sections.content:
* Greezed Lightnin' launch track


 * Greezed Lightnin' loop and spike

fullText:
Greezed Lightnin' was a steel launched shuttle roller coaster located at Six Flags AstroWorld in Houston, Texas, USA.

The roller coaster first opened in 1978 as part of the Western Junction area. The following year the ride's queue received air operated gates.

In 1983, Greezed Lightnin' received re-profiled track and a modified drive panel.

In 1997, Greezed Lightnin' was given a new computer system.

In 2000, the ride received a good refurbishment, with the track and trains being repainted. A new flywheel was also installed, along with a "stop-light" launch system and a Allen-Bradly automation system. The theming was also renovated.

In 2002, Greezed Lightnin' received an ACE plaque honoring the late Anton Schwarzkopf.

In 2003, Greezed Lightnin' celebrated its 25th anniversary.

In 2005, Greezed Lightnin', along with Viper, received seat dividers.

On October 15, 2005, Greezed Lightnin' celebrated its one millionth launch.

It would continue to operate until the permanent closure of Six Flags AstroWorld on October 30, 2005. It was then dismantled.

On January 5, 2006, the ride's control room caught on fire during the ride's disassembly. A section of the queue and station building were destroyed. Extremely hot equipment used for the ride's disassembly was blamed for the fire. The ride suffered no damage.

In January 2006, the ride was sold at auction for $100,000 to David Dean, the co-owner of Joyland Amusement Park. Its mechanical parts and train were planned to be refurbished by Larson International. It was placed in storage about a mile north of Joyland Amusement Park in Lubbock, Texas, USA.

The park originally hoped to reopen the coaster in 2007, but due to a construction delay revealing that the area chosen for installation had wet soil and water four feet under the ground which led to increased cost, the ride was delayed for a 2008 reopening. This would later be pushed back to 2009 following further problems.

The park found it difficult to rebuild the coaster at the park due to the water table the park was located over.

In November 2009, the park abandoned plans to install the ride and listed it for sale for $399,000 on a used ride site. This was later reduced to $299,000 by January 2011.

In 2012, it was announced that Cliff's Amusement Park in Albuquerque, New Mexico, USA would be receiving the roller coaster in exchange for a used SCS waterplay unit going to Joyland, however, the sale was believed to have never took place.

As part of the proposed Grand Texas theme park, Greezed Lightnin' was also to be refurbished and reopened. This also fell through.

In November 2017, the train from Greezed Lightnin' was sold to Frontier City for use on Silver Bullet. The rest of the ride has since been scrapped, with the majority of it being hauled away by late 2018. Only a few remaining parts remain today.

The ride had a single vertical loop which the train passes through forwards and backwards.
